description: Without spending Modal credit, estimate how decomposable real Mathlib proofs are: count theorems with >=2 in-proof nodes under F_named, and sweep the expansion frontier size F_size to see how much shrinking the frontier raises the decomposable count.
motivation: Gate the whole study: if proofs are mostly flat, the granularity axis collapses.
formal_results: 1,715 theorems; under F_named only 68 (~4%) have >=2 in-proof nodes; max dependency-poset depth 3. F_size sweep raises decomposable counts (tau=50->106, 500->224, 2000->348). pi_root vs pi_leaf reconstruction size differs ~22%. Extrapolated ~8k decomposable decls over full Mathlib (~200k) - barely clears the 5-10k threshold.
results_description: Real Mathlib proofs are overwhelmingly flat (~4% decomposable, depth<=3); the granularity axis exists but is marginal.
conclusions: There is just enough decomposable structure (marginally), and the shallow depth means the contrast must be binary, not multi-level.

comments.provenance: data/dryrun_f_size.json is in the working tree but untracked at the pinned commit; the ~4% figure comes from that file (commit the WIP to make it reproducible from git).
